text: The surgeon enters the abdominal cavity using open laparoscopy and places two 5mm trocars in the right and left lumbar regions, along with a 12mm port in the left hypochondrium. Adherences in the supraumbilical midline are released, followed by exploration and dissection of epiploic adhesions to the liver in the upper quadrants.

The surgeon enters the abdominal cavity using open laparoscopy and places two 5mm trocars in the right and left lumbar regions, along with a 12mm port in the left hypochondrium. Adhesions in the supraumbilical midline are released, followed by exploration of the upper quadrants where epiploic adhesions to the liver are dissected. The remnants of the small omentum are opened, revealing the cecum in the left upper quadrant, positioned in front of the stomach. The surgeon begins maneuvers to reduce the hernia by drawing the colon from the right and pushing the cecum from the left.

The surgeon opens the remnants of the small omentum, revealing the cecum occupying the left upper quadrant in front of the stomach. This positioning is likely due to the previous dissection of the gastropathic ligament during the patient's prior hiatal hernia repair.

The surgeon performs maneuvers to reduce the hernia by gently drawing the colon from the right and pushing the cecum from the left. The colon is successfully reduced, revealing the anatomy of the foramen of Winslow, which is located anterior to the inferior vena cava, posterior to the hepatoduodenal ligament, and surrounded by the caudate lobe of the liver. The foramen appears narrow, indicating the herniation was due to abnormal mobility of the ascending colon. The herniated intestinal segment is visibly viable.

The surgeon successfully reduces the herniated colon, revealing the anatomy of the foramen of Winslow, which is located anterior to the inferior vena cava, posterior to the hepatoduodenal ligament, and surrounded by the caudate lobe of the liver. The foramen appears narrow, indicating the herniation was due to abnormal mobility of the ascending colon. The viability of the herniated intestinal segment is confirmed. The cecum is repositioned to its orthoptic location, and the surgeon begins ileocolopexy by securing the mesocolon with multiple cross-stitches of non-absorbable suture up to the right colic angle.

The surgeon secures the mesocolon with multiple cross-stitches of non-absorbable suture up to the right colic angle, performing an ileocolopexy. The abdominal cavity is then washed, and no hemostasis is required. Finally, the foramen of Winslow is packed with diamantum to prevent recurrences.
